nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams and forest pools in the Ucayali drainage; densely shaded, tannin-stained water over soft substrate with leaf litter and submerged roots

decorPreferences: caves (terracotta pots, coconut huts, ceramic caves)
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, stem plants)
blackwater tannin staining (peat filtration or almond leaves)

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild; primarily insects, crustaceans, worms, and zooplankton. In the aquarium, readily accepts frozen and live foods: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, bloodworms, and white worms. High-quality small-format pellets or gel foods are also accepted by conditioned specimens. Varied diet with live or frozen foods is recommended, especially when conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, rummynose tetras)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma males without large territory separation
boisterous fast-moving fish that outcompete for food
large shrimp (fry risk during brood care)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Apistogramma panduro; shows adult male with characteristic blue body coloration.
Wikimedia Commons candidate; verify image exists and license before use.

summary: A striking Peruvian blackwater dwarf cichlid — males display an intense electric-blue body with yellow-and-black finnage. Member of the nijsseni group; closely related to A. nijsseni (fw-fish-205) but occupies a distinct geographic range in the Ucayali drainage. Requires soft, acidic water and cave spawning sites. Haremic; intermediate care.
careNotes:
Apistogramma panduro (described by Römer & Hahn, 1995) is endemic to the Ucayali drainage of Peru, where it inhabits shallow, heavily shaded blackwater streams and floodplain pools. Males are among the most visually spectacular dwarf cichlids in the hobby: the body is suffused with intense electric blue or sky-blue iridescence, contrasting with a black lateral stripe and vivid yellow-and-black dorsal and caudal fin markings. Females are substantially smaller and cryptically yellow-brown with the three-spot 'panda' pattern typical of the nijsseni species group — a dark lateral blotch, dorsal spot, and caudal peduncle spot.

A. panduro is a member of the nijsseni group along with A. nijsseni (fw-fish-205), from which it differs principally in geographic distribution (panduro: Ucayali drainage; nijsseni: Loreto region near Iquitos) and subtle coloration differences in females. The two species should not be mixed in the same tank as they may hybridize.

Soft, acid, tannin-stained blackwater is strongly preferred, with pH 5.0–6.5 and very low hardness (gH 1–6). Hard or alkaline tap water will cause chronic stress and shortened lifespan; RO water reconstituted with a blackwater conditioner and pH buffering is advisable in hard-water areas. Temperature range of 24–29°C is appropriate, with 26–28°C optimal for breeding activity.

A well-decorated aquarium of at least 80 L with a minimum 80 cm footprint is recommended. Multiple caves (ceramic, terracotta, coconut shell) provide essential spawning sites and retreat points. A layer of Indian almond (Terminalia catappa) leaves and driftwood or twisted roots recreates the natural environment, leaches beneficial tannins, and provides shelter. Sandy substrate is preferred.

The species is naturally haremic — one male may be kept with two to three females provided the tank is large enough to allow females to establish separate small territories around individual caves. The male defends a larger territory overlapping several females. A lone pair (1M:1F) is also workable in smaller tanks. Males are territorial toward conspecific males and should not be housed together without very large, heavily decorated tanks.

Compatible with small, peaceful community fish that occupy upper water zones: cardinal and rummynose tetras, pencilfish, otocinclus, and corydoras are classic companions in a biotope-style setup. Avoid boisterous or nippy species that outcompete at feeding time.
breedingNotes:
Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care, characteristic of the genus. The female selects a cave, deposits 40–80 eggs on the interior ceiling, and vigorously defends the clutch and subsequent fry from all tankmates, including the male. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 27°C; larvae become free-swimming 5–7 days later. The female leads and guards the fry school for several weeks.

Breeding is triggered by warm, soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.2, gH 1–4) and conditioning with live or frozen foods. A small water change with slightly cooler, very soft water mimics the rainy-season trigger common to Peruvian Apistogramma. In haremic setups, multiple females may spawn simultaneously with the male. The male should be monitored: if he harasses the guarding female excessively in smaller tanks, temporary separation is advisable. Fry initially accept freshly hatched Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp) and infusoria; micro-pellets can be introduced after 1–2 weeks. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to water chemistry requirements, though the species is more tractable than some blackwater Apistogramma once suitable conditions are achieved.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma panduro
primary.notes: Primary husbandry and taxonomy reference. Provided native range (Ucayali drainage, Peru), water parameters (pH 4.5–6.5, hardness <5 dH, temperature 22–28°C), adult size (males to 7 cm TL, females to 4 cm), habitat description, breeding, and diet notes. Taxonomy: Cichliformes/Cichlidae per modern classification.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma panduro
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichliformes, Cichlidae), native range (Ucayali River basin, Peru), max size (male ~8 cm TL per morphological records), water parameters, and species description date (Römer & Hahn, 1995). Confirms close relationship with A. nijsseni within the nijsseni species group.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guide; cross-checked tank size (20 gal minimum for pairs), water parameters (soft acidic water emphasis), diet (live and frozen foods), haremic social structure, and cave spawning behavior as applicable to A. panduro. No panduro-specific page available.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species profiles
additional.notes: Cross-checked nijsseni-group characteristics (female 3-spot panda pattern, blackwater requirements, cave spawning), geographic separation between panduro (Ucayali) and nijsseni (Loreto/Iquitos), and male blue coloration as species-defining field mark. Breeding temperature and pH triggers confirmed.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Cichlids of Peru
additional.notes: Referenced for geographic range confirmation, nijsseni-group phylogenetic placement, and field collection notes regarding blackwater habitat characteristics (low conductivity, heavy leaf litter, dense canopy shading) in Ucayali tributaries where A. panduro was originally collected.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care; 40–80 eggs per clutch. Requires soft acidic blackwater (pH 5.5–6.2) and live/frozen food conditioning to trigger spawning. Female guards and leads fry; male should be monitored and may need separation in smaller tanks.
